coredata.dc:title: Renal-friendly Li+-doped carbonized polymer dots activate Schwann cell autophagy for promoting peripheral nerve regeneration

coredata.dc:description:
Activation of autophagy in Schwann cells (SCs) has emerged as a powerful trigger for peripheral nerve injury (PNI) repair. Lithium ion (Li+) is a classical autophagy activator that plays an important role in promoting axonal extension and remyelination. However, the therapeutic window of existing lithium drugs is extremely narrow, and the adverse side effects, especially nephrotoxicity, severely limit their therapeutic value. Herein, Li+-doped carbonized polymer dots (Li-CPDs) was synthesized for the first time to change the pharmacokinetics of Li+ from occupying epithelial sodium channels to lipid raft-mediated endocytosis. The in-vivo results confirmed that Li-CPDs could accelerate the removal of myelin debris and promote nerve regeneration via activating autophagy of SCs. Moreover, Li-CPDs exhibited almost no renal toxicity compared to that of raw lithium drugs. Thus, Li-CPDs could serve as a promising Li+-based nanomedicine for PNI regeneration with improved biosafety.
Statement of significance Regardless of the fact that lithium drugs have been used in treatment of mental illness such as manic depression, the systemic side effects and renal metabolic toxicity still seriously restrict their clinical application. Since Li+ and Na+ compete for ion channels of cell membrane, the cell entry efficiency is extremely low and easily affected by body fluctuations, which seems to be an unsolvable problem. Herein, we rationally exploited the endocytotic features of CPDs to develop Li-CPDs. The Li-CPDs improved the entry pathway, greatly reduced nephrotoxicity, and inherited the biological function of Li+ to activate autophagy for promoting peripheral nerve regeneration. Due to the BBB-crossing property of Li-CPDs, it also showed application prospects in future research on central nervous system diseases.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme illustration of the synthetic procedure of Li-CPDs, the protective effect of Li-CPDs on kidneys and the mechanism of Li-CPDs activating autophagy to promote the clearance of myelin debris and accelerate the regeneration of peripheral nerves after injury.

Characterizations of Li-CPDs. (A) TEM image of Li-CPDs (inset shows the high-resolution TEM image and size distribution histogram). (B) The PL spectra of Li-CPDs at different excitation wavelengths. (C) The UV-Vis absorption spectra and the maximum PL excitation and emission spectra of Li-CPDs (inset shows the photographs of Li-CPDs solution under visible light and UV light excitation). (D) FTIR spectra of Li-CPDs. (E) XPS analysis of Li-CPDs, and the deconvolution Li 1s (F), C 1s (G), N 1s (H), and O 1s (I) spectra of Li-CPDs. (J) Representative images of dead/live staining after 24h in RSC96 cells incubated with different concentrations of LC, Li-CPDs. (AM: green, live cells; PI: red, dead cells). Scale bar = 100 μm.

Cellular uptake, intracellular localization and in vivo metabolism of Li-CPDs. (A) Fluorescence images of RSC96 cells incubated with Li-CPDs (green) after treatment with different endocytosis inhibitors (2-DG, CPZ, GEN, M-β-CD, and AMI, blue). (B) Fluorescence images of Li-CPDs (green) colocalized with Mito- and Lyso-tracker (red) in RSC96 cells. (C) Fluorescence intensity of organs after tail vein injection of Li-CPDs at different times. Scale bar = 20 μm.

Li-CPDs avoid lithium ion-induced kidney damage. (A) H&E staining of glomeruli, proximal convoluted tubules (PCTs), distal convoluted tubules (DCTs), and collecting tubes. Scale bar = 50 μm. (B) TUNEL staining of apoptotic cells in kidney tissue (red). Scale bar = 50 μm. (C) SEM images of kidney tissue displayed ultrastructural of Bowman's space in glomeruli and cellular bridging in collecting tubes (1000 ×). Scale bar = 20 μm. (D, E) Quantitative analysis of apoptotic cells. Glomeruli: *P LC (10 mg/d) vs. Control=0.0127, ****P LC (20 mg/d) vs. Control<0.0001; Collecting tubes: ***P LC (10 mg/d) vs. Control=0.0010; ***P LC (20 mg/d) vs. Control=0.0006. The levels of biochemical indicators: CRE (F), and NAG (G) in toxicity experiments. (CRE: no statistically significant difference; NAG: ****P LC (20 mg/d) vs. Control<0.0001).

Li-CPDs promote recovery of motor function after PNI. (A) The squeeze position of the right sciatic nerve of the rats (white arrow). (B) Representive footprints of each group at 7 and 21 d after injury (vertical marker: 10.0 mm; horizontal marker: 5.0 mm). (C) Representative electromyography waves of the gastrocnemius in each group at 21 days after injury. (D, E) Quantitative analysis of the SFI at 7 and 21 d (7 d: no significant difference between groups; 21 d: *P Control vs. LC=0.0105; ****P Control vs. Li-CPDs<0.0001). (F, G) Quantitative analysis of the CMAPs amplitude at 7 and 21 d after injury (7 d: no statistically significant difference; 21 d: **P Control vs. LC=0.0076; ***P Control vs. Li-CPDs=0.0005, ****P N-CPDs vs. Li-CPDs<0.0001). (H) Bilateral fresh gastrocnemius photographs and representative Masson staining images of the mid gastrocnemius at 7, 14, and 21 d after injury (40 ×). Scale bar = 200 μm. (I) Quantitative analysis of wet weight ratio of bilateral gastrocnemius muscle at 21 d after injury (*P Control vs. Li-CPDs=0.0133, **P N-CPDs vs. Li-CPDs=0.0032). (J, K) Quantitative analysis of the mid gastrocnemius muscle fiber area at 7 and 21 d (7 d: no statistically significant difference; 21 d: *P Control vs. Li-CPDs=0.0100, *P N-CPDs vs. Li-CPDs=0.0184).

Li-CPDs promote axon regeneration and remyelination after PNI at 14 and 21 d. (A) Representative IF images of NF200 (red) and MBP (green) at 21 d after injury. DAPI: nuclear staining (blue). Scale bar = 20 μm. (B, C) Quantitative analysis of NF200- and MBP-positive areas of each group (MBP: **P Control vs. LC=0.0015, ****P Control vs. Li-CPDs<0.0001; NF200: **P Control vs. Li-CPDs=0.0084). (D) Representative sciatic nerve images of TBS (Scale bar = 10 μm) and TEM (up: Scale bar = 25 μm; down: Scale bar = 200 nm) at 14 and 21 d after injury . (E, F) Quantitative analysis of the quantity of axons at 14 and 21 d after injury (14 d: **P LC vs. N-CPDs=0.0041, **P N-CPDs vs. Li-CPDs=0.0031; 21d: *P Control vs. LC=0.0350; ***P Control vs. Li-CPDs=0.0007). (G) Quantitative analysis of the regenerated myelin sheath thickness at 21 d after injury (21 d: *P Control vs. Li-CPDs=0.0296).

Li-CPDs enhance myelin debris removal through activating autophagy in SCs at 7 d. (A) TBS, TEM and IF images of the residual myelin debris at 7 d after injury. (B) Quantitative analysis of the degenerated nerve fibers of 7 d after injury (****P Control vs. LC<0.0001; ****P Control vs. Li-CPDs<0.0001; **P LC vs. Li-CPDs=0.021). (C) Quantitative analysis of MBP-immunopositive areas (*P N-CPDs vs. LC=0.0310; *P Control vs. Li-CPDs=0.0154; **P N-CPDs vs. Li-CPDs=0.0036). (D) Representative IF images of S100 and LC3β at 7 d after injury (E) Representative IF images of LC3β and Lamp1 at 7 d after injury. (F, G) Quantitative analysis of the LC3β- and Lamp1-immunopositive regions (LC3β: *P Control vs. Li-CPDs=0.0302; *P N-CPDs vs. Li-CPDs=0.0243. Lamp1: **P Control vs. Li-CPDs=0.0045; ***P LC vs. Li-CPDs=0.0006). (H) Quantitative analysis of the Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) of LC3β and Lamp1 (PCC: ***P Control vs. Li-CPDs=0.0045; ***P LC vs. Li-CPDs=0.0005). Scale bar = 10 μm.

Illustration of Li-CPDs changing the entry pathway of Li+ from occupying Na+ channel to endocytosis and preserving the biological functions of Li+ to elevate the autophagy flux in SCs for accelerating myelin debris clearance. Created with BioRender.com.

head.ce:title: Renal-friendly Li+-doped carbonized polymer dots activate Schwann cell autophagy for promoting peripheral nerve regeneration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Activation of autophagy in Schwann cells (SCs) has emerged as a powerful trigger for peripheral nerve injury (PNI) repair. Lithium ion (Li+) is a classical autophagy activator that plays an important role in promoting axonal extension and remyelination. However, the therapeutic window of existing lithium drugs is extremely narrow, and the adverse side effects, especially nephrotoxicity, severely limit their therapeutic value. Herein, Li+-doped carbonized polymer dots (Li-CPDs) was synthesized for the first time to change the pharmacokinetics of Li+ from occupying epithelial sodium channels to lipid raft-mediated endocytosis. The in-vivo results confirmed that Li-CPDs could accelerate the removal of myelin debris and promote nerve regeneration via activating autophagy of SCs. Moreover, Li-CPDs exhibited almost no renal toxicity compared to that of raw lithium drugs. Thus, Li-CPDs could serve as a promising Li+-based nanomedicine for PNI regeneration with improved biosafety.

Regardless of the fact that lithium drugs have been used in treatment of mental illness such as manic depression, the systemic side effects and renal metabolic toxicity still seriously restrict their clinical application. Since Li+ and Na+ compete for ion channels of cell membrane, the cell entry efficiency is extremely low and easily affected by body fluctuations, which seems to be an unsolvable problem. Herein, we rationally exploited the endocytotic features of CPDs to develop Li-CPDs. The Li-CPDs improved the entry pathway, greatly reduced nephrotoxicity, and inherited the biological function of Li+ to activate autophagy for promoting peripheral nerve regeneration. Due to the BBB-crossing property of Li-CPDs, it also showed application prospects in future research on central nervous system diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Peripheral nerve injury (PNI) is common in modern trauma medicine with high disability rate, and brings a heavy financial burden to society [1]. Unlike the central nervous system (CNS), peripheral nerves possess a vigorous regenerative capacity. However, the long-standing motor dysfunction after PNI continues to be a puzzling problem [2]. The distal axons break down from the nerve stump during PNI, producing numerous myelin debris, which will accumulate in the endoneurium and inhibit further regeneration of nerves [3, 4]. Autophagy, as a precisely regulated debris removal mechanism, is essential for maintaining homeostasis of the neuroregenerative microenvironment [5]. The damaged proteins, organelles, and other autologous components are delivered to lysosomes via the formation of autophagic vesicles, then the degradation products and energy support nerve growth [6]. It has been found that the reduction or absence of autophagic flux in Schwann cells (SCs) is detrimental for myelin debris clearance after nerve damage [7, 8]. Therefore, enhancing the autophagy efficiency of SCs is critical for the promotion of nerve regeneration and the recovery of motor function after PNI.

Lithium ions (Li+) were first reported in the 1950s as mood stabilizer drugs for bipolar disorder [9]. Recent research has revealed that Li+ also has extensive peripheral nerve regeneration promoting effects, the main mechanism of which is accelerating myelin debris clearance through the activation of autophagy [10–12]. However, the unreliable biosafety and extremely narrow therapeutic window of Li+-containing drugs severely limit their clinical applications [13, 14]. This is due to the fact that Li+ enter cells via epithelial sodium ion (Na+) channels, and can directly compete with Na+, thus disrupting the sodium-dependent transport process and inducing acute kidney injury [15–17]. Even if the blood concentrations of Li+ are monitored routinely, the nephrotoxicity and other systemic side effects owing to the heterogeneity of patients are still occur [18, 19]. In this regard, avoiding Na+ channel occupancy and improving the biological functions of Li+ are of great significance for advancing the clinical efficacy of Li+-based drugs.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), as an emerging carbon-based fluorescent nanoparticle, have revealed great prospects in nanomedicine design and drug delivery due to their superior advantages compared to traditional nanomaterials, such as good biocompatibility, high permeability, and easy functionalization [20–23]. It has been widely reported that CPDs enter cells through energy-dependent endocytosis in caveolae- and clathrin-mediated pathways because of the nano-size effect [24, 25]. Therefore, the unique uptake characteristics of CPDs may provide an innovative solution to the bottleneck of lithium drugs. Herein, a new type of Li+-doped carbonized polymer dots (Li-CPDs) was developed as an alternative Li+-containing nanomedicine. The intrinsic fluorescence property of Li-CPDs was used to intuitively confirm that Li-CPDs could be efficiently taken up by cells via endocytosis, thus avoiding the direct occupation of ion channels. Consequently, Li-CPDs exhibited a significantly lower renal toxicity compared to the raw lithium drugs. Moreover, Li-CPDs optimized the biological functions of Li+ to accelerate peripheral nerve regeneration by activating and increasing the level of autophagy in SCs. This study proposed a new method to improve the bioavailability of lithium drugs based on Li-CPDs, which might show broad application prospects as bioactive nanomaterials for nerve regeneration.

Citric acid anhydrous (C6H8O7), lithium citrate tetrahydrate (C6H5Li3O7·4H2O), urea (CH4N2O) were purchased from Aladdin. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the synthesis experiments. Rat Schwann cells (RSC96) were purchased from Chinese Academy of Sciences Cell Bank. Chlorpromazine (CHL), genistein (GEN), methyl-β-cyclodextrin (M-β-CD), 2-deoxy-D-glucose (2-DG), amiloride (AMI) were purchased from Macklin. Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) was purchased from Gibco. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Hyclone.

The Li-CPDs were synthesized via one-step microwave-assisted carbonization reaction. Briefly, 0.5 g of lithium citrate tetrahydrate (LC) and 1 g of urea were mixed in a 100 mL beaker, and dissolved with 10 mL deionized (DI) water under sonication. The mixture solution was heated in a domestic 750 W microwave oven for 5 min, during which the color changed from colorless to dark yellow, and finally brown solid was formed. After cooling to room temperature, 40 mL DI water was poured to fully dissolved the solid. The solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm polyethersulfone membrane and dialyzed with a 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. The solution in the dialysis bag was collected as Li-CPDs. The mass concentration of Li-CPDs solution was calculated by drying a certain volume of solution and weighing. The N-doped CPDs (N-CPDs) were synthesized under the same conditions with citric acid and urea as raw materials, which served as a Li+-free control.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were screened on JEM-2100F, the sample was prepared by dropping 50 μg/mL Li-CPDs solution onto a copper mesh covered with ultra-thin carbon film and dried in air. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra was recorded on Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrophotometer, by configuring 0.5 mg/mL Li-CPDs solution in a transparent quartz cuvette and scaning continuously between 400-560 nm (20 nm interval, slit [3, 3]). Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra was recorded on Shimadzu 3100 spectrophotometer using 0.1 mg/mL Li-CPDs solution with water as reference. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra was performed on Nicolet Avatar 360FTIR spectrophotometer, through dropping Li-CPDs solution into potassium bromide powder and fully drying, then a translucent sheet was prepared to record the transmittance at the wavenumber of 400-4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with a mono X-ray source Al Kα excitation (1486.6 eV), the sample was prepared by spinning a thin layer of Li-CPDs solution on single crystal silicon wafer.

Cytotoxicity assay: The RSC96 cells were cultured in DMEM containing 10% FBS. The cells were incubated with different concentrations of Li-CPDs (0, 0.15 mg/mL, 0.3 mg/mL, 0.75 mg/mL, 1.5 mg/mL, 3 mg/mL) for 24 h. After aspirating the used medium, the cells (5 × 103/100 μL) were treated according to the instructions of the Cell counting kit (CCK8, Beyotime, China), the absorbance at 450 nm was detected with Synergy HT multi-mode microplate reader (BioTek, USA).

Live/dead cell staining: live/dead cell staining was performed by using the Calcein-AM/PI kit (Beyotime, China). Then, 1 μM of Calcein-AM was mixed with 3 μM of PI in buffer. The medium of RSC96 cells containing different concentrations of Li-CPDs (0, 0.3 mg/mL, 1.5 mg/mL, 3 mg/mL) or LC (0, 0.2 mg/mL, 1 mg/mL, 2 mg/mL) was sucked out and then co-incubated with the pre-configured mixture for 30 min. Finally, the fluorescence images were photographed under the fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan). (Green fluorescence channel: Ex/Em=405/500-540nm; Red: Ex/Em=559/600-650nm).

Cellular uptake assay: Various inhibitors of endocytosis including 2-DG (30 mM, ATP synthase inhibitor), CHL (10 μM, lattice protein-mediated endocytosis inhibitor), GEN (200 μM, vesicle-mediated endocytosis inhibitor), M-β-CD (2 mM, lipid raft-mediated endocytosis inhibitor), and AMI (100 μM, macrophage inhibitor) were used to validate the cellular uptake pathways [26]. More specifically, the RSC96 cells were pretreated with endocytosis inhibitors for 1 h. Then, Li-CPDs (1 mg/mL) was added and incubated for 2 h. The RSC96 cells were directly incubated with Li-CPDs (1 mg/mL) for 2 h without endocytosis inhibitors as control. The intrinsic fluorescence properties of Li-CPDs were used to assess uptake by detecting intracellular green fluorescence intensity. Then, RSC96 cells with a density of 2 × 105/mL were cultured in six-well plates. After the cells were adherent, they were incubated with different endocytosis inhibitors for 1 h, and then Li-CPDs were added for 2 h. The honest dye was used to stain nucleus for 30 min. After three times of PBS washing, the cells were digested with trypsin. Six samples of each inhibitor were taken as a repeat. All samples were measured with a flow cytometer at 30 μL/min for a total of 60 s per sample, using a Li-CPDs solution as a control to remove the background.

Intracellular localization assay: the RSC96 cells were pretreated with Li-CPDs (1 mg/mL) for 2 h, and then washed with PBS (10 mM, pH=7.4) for three times. Commercial fluorescent dyes mito-tracker red and lyso-tracker red were used to visualize mitochondria and lysosomes after incubating 30 min, respectively. All of the fluorescence images were obtained by laser confocal microscopy (FV1000, Olympus, Japan) using sequential scanning mode (Green: Ex/Em=488/519 nm; Red: Ex/Em=559/618 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo metabolism and toxicity of Li-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: Sprague Dawley (SD) rats (8 weeks-old male rats weighing 200-220 g) were purchased from Liaoning Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Liaoning, China) and used in the studies of in vivo metabolism of Li-CPDs, organ damage at toxicity doses, and regeneration evaluation of sciatic nerve injury. During the experiments, the welfare of the experimental animals was fully ensured and the pain of the experimental animals was minimized as possible. The animal ethics were approved by the ethics committee from the First Hospital of Jilin University (No. 20220265).

In vivo metabolism assay: nine rats were injected with Li-CPDs (15 mg/mL, 1 mL) via tail vein. The equal volume of saline was use as control. The rats were then euthanized by inhalation of carbon dioxide at different time points (8 h, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 7 days) after intravenous injection, and their organs were harvested. The fluorescence images of tissue were acquired by In-Vivo Xtreme II small animal imaging system (Bruker Corporation, USA).

Toxicity experiment: fifteen rats were divided into five groups and injected with PBS, LC (5, 10 mg/mL, 1mL), Li-CPDs (7.5, 15 mg/mL, 1mL), respectively. The rats were anesthetized after two weeks of continuous intraperitoneal injections (once every 12 h). Arterial blood (5 mL) was extracted from the abdominal aorta, and the upper serum was centrifuged (4°C, 3000 rpm, 10 min) to detect biochemical indicators of organ, including aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), creatinine (CRE), and N-acetyl-β-D-glucosidase (NAG) (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Research Institute).

TUNEL staining: the sections were processed in a series of dewaxing, antigen repair and then labeled for apoptotic cells using the TUNEL Apoptosis Detection Kit (red fluorescence, Servicebio, China) according to the instructions. DAPI (blue fluorescence) was used to stain nuclei. The fluorescence images were obtained by confocal microscopy.

Kidney microstructure assay: the thin slices of 2 mm kidney tissues were sectioned transversely by sharp blade and washed with PBS (pH=7.4). Then, the samples were naturally air dried and sputtered with gold and palladium (4:1) using a coater. The surface microstructure of glomeruli and tubules were observed under scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU-8020, Japan).

The operation Rats were anesthetized by inhalation of 2% isoflurane combined with intraperitoneal injection of ketoprofen (5 mg/kg). The right sciatic nerve was crushed twice in opposite directions (30 s each time) using a Vascular clamp (JZ, China). After that, PBS (20 μL), N-CPDs (15 mg/mL, 20 μL), LC (10 mg/mL, 20 μL) and Li-CPDs (15 mg/mL, 20 μL) were one-dose microinjected into the epineurium distal of the injury. On days 7, 14, and 21, the sciatic nerve at the injury site and gastrocnemius muscle were collected for histological evaluation.

Gait analysis: the hind limb walking trajectories of the rats were obtained based on video system [27]. The video system consists of a walkway, a plane mirror and a camera. The mirrored footprints and lateral lengths were recorded simultaneously from camera (Nikon, Japan). Five lines on the lateral and bottom of the walkway served as horizontal and vertical length references in the video. The sciatic nerve function index (SFI) was calculated by measuring bilateral footprint length (PL), toe extension (TS) (the first to the fifth toe) and intermediate toe extension (IT) (the second to the fourth toe) [28]. The measurements of the left foot (CPL, CTS, CIT) and the corresponding right foot (EPL, ETS, EIT) of each rat were taken into the following formula for calculation:
S F I
=
− 38.3 ×
(
E P L − C P L
)
/ C P L + 109.5 ×
(
E T S − C T S
)
/ C T S
+ 13.3 ×
(
E I T − C I T
)
/ C I T − 8.8

Neuroelectrophysiology analysis: the compound muscle action potentials (CMAPs) of the gastrocnemius were recorded using an electromyography machine (Keypoint, Meridian, Neurolite AG, Switzerland). Specifically, two electrode pins (0.25 × 25 mm) of negligible impedance were fixed as stimulation electrodes with a spacing of 2 mm. Additional electrodes were inserted in the gastrocnemius belly (recording electrode) and the tendon (reference electrode). The CMAPs values were recorded under a current stimulation of 5.8 mA.

Wet weight and Masson staining: the bilateral gastrocnemius of rats were dissected completely and weighed immediately. The tissue sections of the middle gastrocnemius (4 μm) were proceeding to Masson staining according to the manufacturer's protocol (Beyotime, China). The optical images were obtained by light microscopy.

Toluidine blue staining (TBS) and TEM observation: the semithin and ultrathin sections of the sciatic nerves were prepared for the observation of the microscopic morphology of myelin. The prepared semithin sections (500 nm) were used for TBS. The ultrathin sections (100 nm) adhered to copper mesh were treated with 5% uranyl acetate and lead citrate for TEM observation. Finally, the images of the sciatic nerves were shot on light microscopy (Olympus, Japan) and TEM (JEOL, Japan), separately.

Immunofluorescence (IF) assay: the nerve sections were permeabilized with 0.5% trixon for 10 min and 10% fetal bovine serum for 2 h at room temperature. After that, primary antibody NF200 (1:200, Sigma-Aldrich, USA), MBP (1:200, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), S100 (1:200, Novus Biologicals, USA), Lamp1 (1:200, Santa Cruz, British), LC3β (1:200, Abcam, British) were added separately and incubated overnight at 4°C (Table. S1). After washing 3 times with PBS, secondary antibodies AF488 (1:1000, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), FITC (1:1000, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) and AF594 (1:1000, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) were used to incubated for 2 h. The IF images were obtained in sequential scanning mode (Blue fluorescence channel: Ex/Em=405/461 nm; Green: Ex/Em=488/519 nm; Red: Ex/Em=559/618 nm) on the laser confocal microscope.

The image processing involved in this study was procedure with Image J (NIH, USA). All data were analyzed by Origin 2022 (Origin Lab, California, United States) and Graphpad prism (GraphPad Software, USA). The data that passed the normal distribution test were further analyzed with One-way ANOVA test; for the data with non-normal distribution, the Wilcoxon test was used. All the result were showed as mean ± standard deviation (M±SD), and P<0.05 were considered statistically significant (ns: P>0.05, *: P<0.05, **: P<0.01, ***: P<0.001, ****: P<0.001).

Next, Fourier-transformed infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed to investigate the chemical composition and surface structure of the Li-CPDs. The stretching vibration of O-H/N-H at 3184 cm−1, C=O at 1713 cm−1, C=N at 1400 cm−1, and C-O/C-N/C-S at 1188 cm−1 were observed in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2D), indicating abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of the Li-CPDs [31]. The XPS analysis confirmed that the elemental composition of Li-CPDs included C, N, O, and Li (Fig. 2E). According to the peak intensity ratio, the atomic percentages were calculated as 39.8%, 10.8%, 34.8%, and 14.6% for C, N, O, and Li, respectively. The peaks around the binding energy of 57.1 eV in the deconvoluted Li 1s spectra were attributed to the Li+ states (Fig. 2F) [32], further demonstrating the Li+ doping within Li-CPDs. Consistent with the FTIR results, there were four types of carbon bonds in the C 1s spectra, i.e., C-C/C=C, C-N, C=O, and C-O at 286.6 eV, 287.8 eV, 290.3 eV, and 294.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 2G) [33]. The typical pyrrolic/graphitic nitrogen and C-O/C=O signatures were also found in the N 1s and O 1s spectra, similar to the previous reports of CPDs (Fig. 2H and 2I) [34]. Moreover, the content of Li+ was accurately determined as 9.56583 mg/L (0.2 mg/mL of Li-CPDs) by inductively coupled plasma emission spectroscopy (ICP) (Fig. S1), which served as the concentration reference for the control experiments with LC. The above results proved that the fluorescent Li-CPDs were successfully developed and might serve as a Li+-containing nanodrug.

To assess the cytocompatibility of Li-CPDs prior to in-vivo applications, the RSC96 cells were cultured with different concentrations of Li-CPDs for 24 h. In the standard CCK8 assay, the cell survival rates of the Li-CPD groups were higher than 80% even at a high concentration (3 mg/mL) (Fig. S2). Moreover, the live and dead cells were intuitively differentiated by calcein AM (green fluorescence) and PI (red fluorescence) co-staining after treatment with LC and Li-CPDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2J, preincubation of RSC96 cells with different Li-CPD concentrations did not induce obvious apoptosis, suggesting the negligible cytotoxicity of Li-CPDs. In contrast, LC treatment showed a greater amount of cell death. Overall, the Li-CPDs possessed lower cytotoxicity compared to the raw lithium drug LC. Before verifying whether Li-CPDs could promote nerve regeneration, cell cloning experiments and neurofilament protein (NF200) axon staining were performed, which illustrated that Li-CPDs group promoted the proliferation of RSC96 cells and axonal extension (Fig. S3 and S4). Therefore, it was reasonable to believe that Li-CPDs inherited the positive biological functions of Li+, but with improved biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake pathways and intracellular location of Li-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have revealed that CPDs enter cells via energy-dependent endocytosis in caveolae- and clathrin-mediated pathways due to the nano-size effect [35]. In this work, the intrinsic green fluorescence of Li-CPDs was utilized as an indicator to verify the internalization and uptake efficiency of Li-CPDs by RSC96 cells under the influence of different chemical endocytosis inhibitors. As shown in Fig. 3 A, after pretreatment with 2-deoxy-D-glucose (2-DG, an ATP synthase inhibitor) for 1 h, there was almost no fluorescence signal of Li-CPDs existed in cells, indicating that its cell entry process was energy dependent. Besides, the cellular uptake of Li-CPDs was significantly decreased with the addition of methyl-β-cyclodextrin (M-β-CD, a lipid raft-mediated endocytosis inhibitor), suggesting that Li-CPDs enter cells mainly by lipid raft-mediated endocytosis. Moreover, amiloride (AMI, a macrophage inhibitor), genistein (GEN, a vesicle-mediated endocytosis inhibitor), and chlorpromazine (CPZ, a lattice protein-mediated endocytosis inhibitor) also inhibited the internalization of Li-CPDs, but to a lesser extent, which further demonstrated that lipid-mediated endocytosis was the leading pathway for Li-CPDs to efficiently penetrate cell membranes. In addition, the 3D map of the confocal fluorescence intensity at each location of the cells and flow cytometry data further confirmed the above conclusion (Fig. S5 and S6).

To further investigate the organelle localization of Li-CPDs, Mito- and Lyso-trackers were utilized for fluorescence colocalization of Li-CPDs with mitochondria and lysosomes, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, Li-CPDs were primarily enriched in the lysosome, which was consistent with the previously reported subcellular location of CPDs [36]. Taken together, our results support that the entry pathway of Li-CPDs had converted from Na+ channel occupancy by Li+ to the efficient endocytosis and lysosomal phagocytosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organ distribution and renal biocompatibility of Li-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on their intrinsic fluorescence property, the in-vivo metabolism of Li-CPDs was clarified by detecting the fluorescence biodistribution in organs (Fig. 3C). It could be seen that Li-CPDs were present in all organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, brain, and intestine) at 8 h after one-dose tail vein injection, and the fluorescence intensity began to decrease after 24 h. The highly visible fluorescence found in the brain tissue originated from the blood-brain barrier (BBB) permeability of CPDs, as reported in many literatures [22, 37, 38], indicating that Li-CPDs might have research potential in central nervous system (CNS)-related diseases. Then, Li-CPDs were only detected in the kidneys and intestines at 48 h, and were completely excreted by 72 h. These results suggested that renal and intestinal were probably the main metabolic organs of Li-CPDs.

Since it has become the consensus that the most common side effect of Li+ is nephrotoxicity [17], the kidney damage from LC (10 and 20 mg/d) and Li-CPDs (15 and 30 mg/d) was assessed by renal pathology after 14 d of consecutive intraperitoneal administration. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the morphology of the glomeruli and collecting tubes treated with Li-CPDs (15 and 30 mg/d) was not significantly different from that of the control group. In contrast, cell vacuolization and bridging were evident in the collection tubes of the LC group (20 mg/d), indicating the renal toxicity of Li+ [39], while no obvious lesions and inflammatory infiltration were observed in other tissues (heart, liver, spleen, lung) (Fig. S7). To further investigate the cellular viability of renal tissues, TUNEL staining was used to label the apoptotic cells, which revealed that there were fewer apoptotic cells within the glomeruli of the Li-CPDs group than that of the LC group at both concentrations (Fig. 4B). Specifically, the proportion of apoptotic cells in the LC group reached 3.5±4.2% and 10.6±5.4% at the concentrations of 10 mg/d and 20 mg/d, respectively, which were significantly greater than that of the control group (1.8±1.1%). However, there were no statistical differences in the apoptosis ratio between the Li-CPDs groups (0.8±1.0 % for 15 mg/d and 0.5±0.2% for 30 mg/d) and the control group (Fig. 4D). Similarly, in the sections of renal collecting tubes, the difference in the apoptosis percentage among the Li-CPDs groups (6.6±5.7% for 15 mg/d and 5.6±7.0% for 30 mg/d) and the control group (1.0±0.7%) was not statistically significant, whereas even the low concentration of LC (10 mg/d) resulted in a obviously increase of apoptotic cells to 14.0±10.6% (Fig. 4E). That is to say, Li-CPDs exhibited lower renal glomerular and tubular damage than LC, showing better kidney friendliness.

Next, slices of renal tissue were prepared for SEM observation to further visualize the variations of the surface microstructure. There were no obvious morphological changes in the glomeruli among the Li-CPDs groups and the control group (Fig. 4C). However, the Bowman spaces (arrows) of the LC group (20 mg/d) was significantly dilated. Also, the morphology of renal collecting tubes of the 10 mg/d LC group started to appear cell bridging and enlarged inter-tubular gaps, which were more obvious with a higher LC concentration (20 mg/d). In other words, LC showed higher nephrotoxicity than Li-CPDs in a concentration-dependent manner, which was consistent with TUNEL and H&E staining results. Furthermore, typical biochemical parameters were introduced to evaluate the liver and kidney functions. As shown in Fig. 4F and S8, the levels of alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and creatinine (CRE), reflecting hepatocyte injury and glomerular filtration capacity, did not change significantly at high concentrations of the LC and Li-CPDs groups. However, N-acetyl-β-D-glucosidase (NAG), an acute tubular injury marker [40], was substantially increased after treatment with 20 mg/d LC (45.4±3.2 U/L) compared to that of the control group (30.6±2.8 U/L) (Fig. 4G), while Li-CPDs at the same concentration hardly elevated the level of NAG (32.8±2.4 U/L). In summary, the as-synthesized Li-CPDs possessed greatly improved biocompatibility and renal safety, supporting the validity of our strategy to transform the Li+ entry pathway.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional recovery assessment after treatment with Li-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess whether the applied Li-CPDs inherited the biological functions of Li+ to promote recovery of motor function after PNI, a model of sciatic nerve crush in rats was established (Fig. 5 A). The gait tracking data on days 7 and 21 after injury were recorded, and the Sciatic Function Index (SFI) was calculated based on the footprints from the video gait system to quantify the degree of functional recovery. In general, sciatic nerve injury manifested as elongated footprints and retracted toes, while the footprints gradually returned to normal as damaged nerves regenerated and muscle function recovered (Fig. 5B) [41]. The quantitative analysis of SFI revealed no significant differences in all the groups on 7 d after injury because early restoration of motor functions had not progressed (Fig. 5D). However, the SFI value of the Li-CPD group (-15.77) was relatively higher than those of the control group (-38.03), the N-CPDs group (-36.61), and the LC group (-23.86) after 21 d, indicating better motor functional improvements (Fig. 5E).

The electrophysiological test was a critical and accurate method to assess the functional recovery after nerve injury (Fig. 5C). The amplitude of compound muscle action potentials (CMAPs) was proportional to the amount of effective nerve electrical conduction, and served as the reliable index for judging functional recovery indeed derived from the regeneration of the innervated nerves [42]. As expected, there were almost no variations in the values of CMAPs after 7 d (Fig. 5F), while the average amplitude of the Li-CPDs group reached the highest value among all the groups at 21 days after injury (Fig. 5G). Combined with the results of the SFI and CMAPs, it was concluded that Li-CPDs accelerated nerve regeneration and thus promoted function restoration after sciatic nerve injury. Furthermore, PNI could cause gastrocnemius atrophy and timely innervation of muscle was essential for the maintenaning of muscle structure and function [43]. By comparing the appearance of the bilateral gastrocnemius and the cross-sectional area of the muscle fibers, pronounced muscle atrophy was observed in the N-CPDs and control groups (Fig. 5H). The quantitative analysis of the gastrocnemius muscle wet weight and bundle area also confirmed the therapeutic effect of Li-CPDs on sciatic nerve injury at 21 d (Fig. 5I, 5J, 5K and S9). In addition, the motor endplate of neuroskeletal muscle was evaluated by acetylcholine staining, it was found that the staining sites in gastrocnemius tissue sections were significantly increased after Li-CPDs treatment (Fig. S10), which further suggested that Li-CPDs facilitated reinnervation of target muscles. Based on the above results, it was completely verified that Li-CPDs could retain Li+ biological activities and promote peripheral nerve regeneration, from the electrical conduction of the sciatic nerve across the injury site, to the function of the target organ gastrocnemius muscle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological evaluation of regenerated nerves after treatment with Li-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: To determine whether the synthetic Li-CPDs were capable of facilitating remyelination as reported for the neuroprotective effects of lithium salts [44], the immunofluorescence (IF) staining of myelin basic protein (MBP) and NF200 were performed distal to the injured site. As shown in Fig. 6 A, NF200 and MBP were sparsely distributed in the transverse nerve section of the control group, indicating that axonal regeneration was delayed after injury. However, in the LC group and the Li-CPDs group, NF200 showed a uniform and compact appearance with MBP continuously wrapping the axons, which represented that both LC and Li-CPDs could accelerate neuroregeneration by promoting remyelination. It was worth mentioning that there were no differences between the control and the Li+-free N-CPDs groups, proving that the function of Li-CPDs originated from the doped Li+. Also, the quantitative analysis of NF200- and MBP-immunoreactive positive areas in the four groups further confirmed the above conclusion (Fig. 6B and 6C).

Next, toluidine blue staining (TBS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were utilized for labeling myelin sheaths and observing nerve ultrastructure [45]. As shown in Fig. 6D, loose and coiled degenerated myelin coexisted with newly grown myelin at 14 d, in which the nascent axons of the Li-CPDs and LC groups were obviously more densely wrapped with myelin at 21 d after injury, which was determined by the calculated G-ratio value (Fig. S11). Moreover, the quantitative analysis of myelin thickness and number further revealed that the regenerated myelin sheaths in the Li-CPDs group were thicker and more numerous than other groups at 21 d, respectively (Fig. 6F and 6G). Interetingsly, a distinct reversal in the myelin number was noticed in the Li-CPDs group at 14 and 21 days after PNI (Fig. 6E and 6F). It was reasonable to speculate that Li-CPDs accelerated the clearance of myelin debris, which was manifested as a decrease in the number of degraded axons at 14 d, and this played a key role in the increased regeneration speed at the later stage (21 d) due to the improved regenerative microenvironment, resulting in a significant rebound in the nascent myelin number. To further prove our hypothesis, early neuromorphological assessments and mechanistic studies were carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of early autophagy in SCs after treatment with Li-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: The clearance of myelin debris by SCs within 5-7 days after injury was a prerequisite for subsequent neurogenesis [5]. During the early stage (7 d), the TBS staining results showed that the application of Li-CPDs effectively reduced the degenerated myelin and replaced it with newly formed thin myelin sheaths (Fig. 7 A). Meanwhile, the immunohistochemical staining of Ki67 on injured nerves displayed that Li-CPDs also significantly increased SCs proliferation, which was beneficial for the initial preparation of nerve regeneration (Fig. S12). In addition, the representative TEM images displayed typical collapsed myelin sheaths, ongoing degradation in the control, N-CPDs groups, whereas the produced myelin debris were completely cleared in the Li-CPDs group. Moreover, the quantitative analysis also determined that compared with the control and N-CPDs groups, the Li-CPDs group contained significantly fewer degenerated nerve fibers (Fig. 7B). Similar results were obtained from the IF staining of MBP (Fig. 7A and 7C), further indicatng that the administration of Li-CPDs could promote myelin debris removal.

The biogenesis and consumption of autophagosomes in SCs could be divided into five separate steps: induction, expansion, vesicle completion, fusion, and cargo degradation during Wallerian degeneration [7, 46]. When the autophagosomes underwent the transition process from expansion to vesicle completion, the microtubule-associated protein (LC3β) protein, which was located in the inner bilayer and flipped to the outer membrane of autophagosomes [47]. Thus, LC3β was recognized as a vital autophagy marker. Based on this, double IF staining of S100 (a SC marker protein) and LC3β was introduced to determine whether treatment with Li-CPDs could activate SCs autophagy. As shown in Fig. 7D, there were plenty of LC3β-positive punctate structures merged with S100 in the LC and Li-CPDs groups, while there were few in the control and N-CPDs groups. Also, the quantitative analysis verified that treatment with Li-CPDs increased the area of LC3β immunoreactivity to 21.2% after injury (Fig. 7F). These results further confirmed our hypothesis that Li-CPDs could enhance myelin debris removal after PNI by upregulating SCs autophagy.

What's more, increased autophagic vesicles in SCs could also be the impaired fusion of autophagosomes with lysosomes [48]. In this regard, double IF staining of lysosome-associated membrane protein 1 (Lamp1) and LC3β was conducted to further distinguish this possibility. As shown in Fig. 7E, Lamp1 and LC3β were distributed compactly in the cytoplasm and partially overlapped in the Li-CPDs group. Compared to the control and LC groups, there were more Lamp1-immunoreactive regions in the Li-CPDs group (Fig. 7G), that is, treatment with Li-CPDs efficiently increased the number of lysosomes, and the lysosomes were spatially fused with autophagosomes (Fig. 7H). In conclusion, treatment with Li-CPDs after PNI could activate autophagy and increase autophagic flux in SCs, which would greatly speed up the early myelin debris clearance process, thereby promoting nerve regeneration and motor function recovery. Especially, changing the entry pathways of Li+ from occupying Na+ channels into efficient endocytosis of Li-CPDs would be beneficial to greatly improve the drug utilization and biocompatibility (Fig. 8 ).

In summary, Li-CPDs were rationally designed and successfully synthesized to serve as an alternative for traditional lithium drugs. In vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrated that Li-CPDs altered the entry pathway of Li+ from occupying Na+ channels to endocytosis, meanwhile preserving the biological functions of Li+ without renal toxicity. Treatment with Li-CPDs could significantly increase the autophagy flux of SCs and facilitate nerve regeneration after sciatic nerve injury, ultimately promoting the recovery of motor function. In particular, the metabolic distribution of Li-CPDs in the brain tissue was observed, providing the basis for its exploitation in CNS-related diseases, which requires further validation in future work. This study not only provides a simple, low-cost, and effective strategy for improving lithium-containing drugs, but it also heralds the promising application of Li-CPDs on peripheral nerve injury repair, thus offering new therapeutic options for neuroregeneration.
